description: Explains pytest's purpose as a Python testing framework, its public entry points, documentation shape, and the high-level path from CLI invocation through configuration, session setup, collection, and test execution.
document:
pytest is a Python testing framework whose public identity is centered on making small tests easy to write while still scaling to complex functional testing for applications and libraries. The README demonstrates this with a tiny failing test written using a plain assert statement, then shows pytest reporting the failure with assertion introspection rather than requiring a special assertion method family. The feature list frames the project around automatic discovery of tests, modular fixtures for reusable test resources, compatibility with unittest and trial suites, and a rich plugin architecture with a large external plugin ecosystem. The package docstring in `src/pytest/__init__.py` gives the short version of that purpose: unit and functional testing with Python.

The documentation layout reinforces that pytest is both a user-facing tool and an extensible framework. The full documentation starts with getting-started material, then splits into how-to guides for daily testing tasks, reference pages for exact APIs, explanation pages for concepts such as test discovery and fixtures, and further material for examples, compatibility, contributing, history, and release notes. That organization matches the README's promise: a beginner can run a simple test quickly, while a project with custom fixtures, plugins, marks, collection behavior, or existing test suites can still find deeper guidance.

The top-level package is the stable convenience surface for test authors and integrators. `pytest.__init__` imports and re-exports the commonly used functions, decorators, classes, warnings, reports, node types, configuration objects, and command entry points from the internal implementation packages. This means users normally import the public pytest namespace for helpers such as fixtures, marks, skipping, expected failures, approximate comparisons, exception assertions, warning assertions, and in-process test execution, instead of reaching into private modules. The same file also exposes debugging support through a set-trace alias and version metadata, making the package namespace the ergonomic front door to the framework.

Command-line execution is intentionally thin at the outermost layer. `src/pytest/__main__.py` exists so running the package as a module delegates to `pytest.console_main()` and exits with its returned status. The console entry point in the configuration package is described as the CLI entry point and not the programmable API; code that wants an in-process run should use `pytest.main()` instead. The console path calls the in-process runner, flushes standard output, and handles broken pipes by redirecting remaining output away from stdout so interpreter shutdown does not raise another pipe error.

The in-process runner is responsible for converting command arguments and plugins into a configured pytest run. It accepts explicit arguments or falls back to the process command line, handles a simple version request early without starting the full infrastructure, temporarily publishes the pytest version in the environment, prepares configuration, invokes the command-line hook, normalizes known exit codes, and always unconfigures before returning. Configuration preparation creates a plugin manager with default plugins, registers any supplied plugin objects or plugin names, and asks the hook system to parse the command line. Usage problems and conftest import failures are reported as usage errors rather than ordinary test failures.

The default command protocol lives in `src/_pytest/main.py`. The command-line hook delegates to `wrap_session`, which creates the root `Session`, configures pytest, fires session start and finish hooks, restores the invocation directory, and maps interruptions, failed tests, internal errors, and explicit exits to exit statuses. The session object is the root of the collection tree and keeps the run's core counters and item list. The default main routine first triggers collection, then the runtest loop, and finally reports failure if tests failed, no-tests if nothing was collected, or success otherwise. In that shape, pytest's purpose becomes concrete: the public API and CLI lead into a plugin-aware session engine that discovers tests, runs them, reports useful failures, and exits with a status suitable for automation.
